title: Two Sum
problem:
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return the indices of the two numbers such that they add up to `target`.

You may assume each input has exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.
example:
Input: nums = [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9
Output: [0, 1]  (because nums[0] + nums[1] == 9)
hint: Use a hash map to store numbers you've seen and their indices. For each number, check if (target - current) is already in the map.
explanation: We trade space for time — instead of checking every pair (O(n²)), we remember what we've seen and check complements in O(1).

title: Reverse a String
problem: Reverse a string without using the built-in `.reverse()` method.
example:
Input: "hello"
Output: "olleh"
hint: Use two pointers — one at the start, one at the end — and swap characters moving toward the center.
explanation: Two-pointer technique — halve the work by going from both ends. Same pattern works for reversing arrays in place.

title: Palindrome Check
problem: Check if a string is a palindrome, ignoring case, spaces, and non-alphanumeric characters.
example:
Input: "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"
Output: true
hint: Sanitize the string first (lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric), then use two pointers from both ends.
explanation: Cleaning the string first makes the comparison straightforward. Two-pointer avoids creating a reversed copy.

title: FizzBuzz
problem: Print numbers 1 to 100. For multiples of 3, print 'Fizz' instead of the number. For multiples of 5, print 'Buzz'. For multiples of both 3 and 5, print 'FizzBuzz'.
example: 1, 2, Fizz, 4, Buzz, Fizz, 7, 8, Fizz, Buzz, 11, Fizz, 13, 14, FizzBuzz, 16, ...
hint: Check the most specific case (both 3 AND 5) first, then the individual cases.
explanation: Build the output string instead of using nested if/else. Cleaner and extends easily (e.g., add 'Jazz' for multiples of 7).

title: Valid Parentheses
problem: Given a string containing just the characters `(`, `)`, `{`, `}`, `[`, `]`, determine if the input string is valid. Open brackets must be closed by the same type, in the correct order.
example:
Input: "()[]{}"
Output: true

Input: "([)]"
Output: false
hint: Use a stack. Push opening brackets, pop when you see a closing bracket and check it matches.
explanation: Stacks are perfect for nested/matching problems. Last-opened bracket must be first-closed — LIFO.

title: Find Maximum in Array
problem: Find the maximum value in an array without using `Math.max()` or `.sort()`.
example:
Input: [3, 1, 7, 2, 9, 4]
Output: 9
hint: Initialize with the first element, then iterate and update when you find something bigger.
explanation: Linear scan is optimal — you can't find the max without looking at every element at least once.

title: Count Vowels
problem: Count the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u) in a string. Case-insensitive.
example:
Input: "Hello World"
Output: 3  (e, o, o)
hint: Match against a vowel regex, or iterate and check membership in a Set.
explanation: Two valid approaches. Regex is concise; Set is faster because Set.has() is O(1) and there's no regex compilation.

title: Merge Two Sorted Arrays
problem: Given two sorted arrays `a` and `b`, merge them into a single sorted array.
hint: Use two pointers — one per array. Compare current elements, push the smaller one, advance that pointer.
explanation: Exploits the fact that inputs are already sorted. This is the merge step of merge sort.

title: Anagram Check
problem: Check if two strings are anagrams (contain the same characters with the same frequencies).
example:
Input: "listen", "silent"
Output: true

Input: "hello", "world"
Output: false
hint: Sort both strings and compare. Or, count character frequencies with a Map.
explanation: Sorting is O(n log n) but very concise. Frequency counting is O(n) but more code. Pick based on input size.

title: Factorial
problem: Compute n! (n factorial) — the product of all positive integers up to n. Note: 0! = 1.
hint: Either recursively (n × factorial(n-1), base case 0! = 1) or iteratively with a running product.
explanation: Recursion is elegant but for large n, iterative is safer (JavaScript has a call stack limit around 10,000).

title: Fibonacci (Efficient)
problem: Return the nth Fibonacci number. F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2). Handle n up to 50 efficiently.
example:
Input: 10
Output: 55
hint: Naive recursion is O(2^n) — way too slow. Memoize, or iterate bottom-up keeping only the last two values.
explanation: Bottom-up with two variables is optimal — O(n) time, O(1) space. Memoization is O(n) time but O(n) space.

title: Binary Search
problem: Given a sorted array and a target value, return the index of the target (or -1 if not found). Must be O(log n) time.
example:
Input: nums = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11], target = 7
Output: 3
hint: Maintain left and right pointers. Compute mid, compare, halve the search space each iteration.
explanation: Halving the search space each iteration gives O(log n). The bit-shift `>> 1` is a fast integer divide by 2.

title: Remove Duplicates from Array
problem: Return a new array with duplicates removed, preserving the order of first occurrence.
hint: Use a Set to track seen elements, or just convert to Set and back to array.
explanation: Sets guarantee uniqueness. The second approach is more flexible — works for objects via a key function.

title: Debounce Function
problem: Implement a `debounce(func, wait)` function that delays invoking `func` until `wait` ms have elapsed since the last call.
hint: Use setTimeout. Clear the previous timer on each call so only the last one fires.
explanation: Closures over `timer` let us share state across calls. Essential for search-as-you-type, resize handlers, etc.

title: Throttle Function
problem: Implement a `throttle(func, limit)` function that ensures `func` is called at most once per `limit` ms.
example:
const log = throttle(console.log, 1000);
// Calling log() 10 times in 1 second → log runs only once (or twice, edge case)
hint: Track the last invocation time. If now - last >= limit, call it; otherwise skip (or schedule a trailing call).
explanation: Unlike debounce (which waits for silence), throttle guarantees a max rate. Perfect for scroll/mousemove handlers.

title: Deep Clone Object
problem: Deep-clone an object (including nested objects and arrays) without using `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify())` or `structuredClone()`.
example:
Input: { a: 1, b: { c: 2, d: [3, 4] } }
Output: a new object with the same structure but no shared references
hint: Recursively clone. Handle arrays, plain objects, and primitives. Watch out for null (typeof null === 'object').
explanation: Handles the common cases. Real-world deep clones also need to handle Dates, Maps, Sets, RegExps, circular refs — that's why `structuredClone()` exists in modern JS.

title: Flatten Nested Array
problem: Flatten a deeply nested array into a single-level array.
hint: Use recursion. If an element is an array, recurse; otherwise, push it to the result.
explanation: Recursion handles arbitrary depth. The built-in `.flat(Infinity)` does the same thing — know both!

title: Capitalise Each Word
problem: Given a sentence, capitalise the first letter of each word.
example:
Input: "the quick brown fox"
Output: "The Quick Brown Fox"
hint: Split by space, map each word to its capitalised version, join back with spaces.
explanation: Clean functional approach with split/map/join. The same pattern works for many string transformations.

title: Count Character Frequencies
problem: Return an object mapping each character in a string to its frequency. Case-sensitive.
example:
Input: "hello"
Output: { h: 1, e: 1, l: 2, o: 1 }
hint: Iterate and use a plain object or Map. Initialize missing keys to 0 then increment, or use nullish coalescing.
explanation: Frequency counters are a fundamental pattern — used in anagram checks, compression, stats, etc.

title: Sum of Digits
problem: Given a non-negative integer, return the sum of its digits.
hint: Either convert to string and iterate, or repeatedly mod 10 + divide by 10.
explanation: The math approach is faster (no string allocation) and works in any language. String approach is more readable.
